worth_it_explanation: Reporting accuracy on Amazon Publisher Services rests on two questions a publisher can answer from records they already hold. The first is whether the revenue and fill the console reports reconcile against the money that actually arrives because a gap between the two means a number somewhere is wrong. The second is whether the report dimensions and the timezone are set so the figures line up with the ad server and the payment statement rather than being compared across mismatched definitions. This check is a review of reporting configuration and a reconciliation against records the publisher can pull, so it costs reading time rather than a rebuild, and the finding points to a specific discrepancy the publisher can trace and close.
intro: Amazon Publisher Services (APS) runs server-side header bidding through Transparent Ad Marketplace (TAM) and Unified Ad Marketplace (UAM), and the numbers it reports drive the decisions a publisher makes about that demand. This subcategory is about whether those numbers can be trusted, not about the yield settings a separate check covers. These checks confirm that the revenue and fill the console shows reconcile against the payments that arrive, and that report dimensions and the timezone are configured so a figure from APS can be compared against the ad server and the payment statement on the same basis rather than across definitions that do not match.

audit_checks.title: Reported revenue and fill are not reconciled against actual payments
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Console revenue is an estimate built from auction records, while the payment statement is the money that arrived after invalid-traffic deductions, currency conversion, and any post-auction adjustment. A publisher who never reconciles the two cannot tell whether a gap is a normal timing difference or a real shortfall, so a discrepancy that signals a tracking loss or a billing error can sit unexamined while decisions are made on a revenue figure that does not match the deposit.

educate.base:
The revenue Amazon Publisher Services (APS) shows in its console is an estimate. It is built from the auction records Transparent Ad Marketplace (TAM) and Unified Ad Marketplace (UAM) write at the moment each impression clears, before the deductions and adjustments that happen between the auction and the deposit. The money that actually arrives is the figure on the payment statement, and it can differ from the estimate for reasons that are normal as well as reasons that are not. Reconciling the two is how a publisher tells the difference.

Several steps sit between the estimated revenue and the paid revenue. Invalid traffic is filtered out, so impressions counted at auction time can be removed from the billable total later. Demand partners settle on their own cycles, so revenue booked in one month can be paid in the next. Currency conversion moves the figure when a buyer pays in one currency and the publisher is paid in another. A demand partner can also issue a post-auction adjustment, a clawback, or a discrepancy credit that the console estimate never saw. Each of these can open a gap between what the console reported and what the bank received without anything being broken.

The audit question is not whether a gap exists, given that some gap almost always does. The question is whether the publisher knows the size of the usual gap, can explain it, and would notice if it changed. A reconciliation that compares console revenue against the payment statement for the same period, broken out by demand partner where the statements allow it, turns the gap into a tracked number rather than an unexamined difference. When the gap widens beyond its usual range, that is the signal worth tracing because it can point to a measurement loss, a partner that underpaid, or a billing error rather than the normal settling of accounts.

A few signs point to revenue and fill that are not reconciled against payments:
  • No one compares the console revenue figure against the payment statement for the same period, so the size of the usual gap is unknown
  • The fill rate the console reports has not been checked against the impressions the ad server recorded as filled, so a discrepancy between the two systems would go unseen
  • A revenue shortfall was noticed only when a deposit looked low, rather than caught by a routine reconciliation
  • Demand-partner statements are filed without being matched against the per-partner revenue the console attributed to each
You may want to confirm that the console revenue and fill reconcile against the money that actually arrives. One approach is to pull the console figures and the payment statements for the same closed period, match them by demand partner where the statements allow, and record the gap and what explains it so a future change in that gap is visible.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, revenue can swing hard by section and by season, so a blended monthly reconciliation can hide a gap that lives on one slice of inventory. You may want to reconcile console revenue against payments broken out by the demand partners and sections that carry the most revenue, so a shortfall on a high-value slice is visible rather than averaged away across the whole site.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, sponsorship and direct-sold revenue often sit alongside the programmatic revenue APS reports, and the payment statements can mix them. You may want to separate the APS programmatic revenue from the direct-sold lines before reconciling, so the comparison matches the console figure against the payment for the same inventory rather than against a combined total.

learn_more.source_title: Optimizing Header Bidding Integrations on Transparent Ad Marketplace

educate.learn_more_note: Amazon Publisher Services's detailed reporting and payment-reconciliation documentation, including how its console attributes revenue per demand partner, is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the console revenue and fill against the payment statements for the same closed period, matched by demand partner where the statements allow, and record the gap and what explains it.

aps_console.label: Amazon Publisher Services console
steps: Open the Transparent Ad Marketplace or Unified Ad Marketplace reporting and pull the estimated revenue, the impressions, and the fill rate for a closed period that has already been paid.
Break the revenue out by demand partner where the console allows, so the figures can be matched against the partner statements.
Record the console totals and the per-partner figures for the period being reconciled.

payment_statements.label: Payment statements and ad server
steps: Pull the payment statement for the same closed period and, where the statement itemizes by demand partner, line up each partner's paid amount against the console figure.
Pull the ad server's filled-impression count for the inventory APS served and compare it against the fill the console reported, so a discrepancy between the two systems is visible.

Record the gap between estimated and paid revenue, the gap between console fill and ad-server fill, and note what explains each, such as invalid-traffic deductions, settlement timing, currency conversion, or a post-auction adjustment.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the console revenue and fill, the paid revenue from the statement, and the ad-server filled-impression count for the same closed period, matched by demand partner where the statements allow. A setup where the gap between estimated and paid revenue is known, explained, and within its usual range, and where console fill lines up with ad-server fill, passes. A setup where no reconciliation exists, where the gap is unexplained, or where the gap has widened beyond its usual range without a reason on record is a fail recorded with the period and partners affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the console revenue and fill, the paid revenue from the statement, and the ad-server fill for the same period above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not reconcile the revenue and fill Amazon Publisher Services (APS) reports against the payments that actually arrive, so the size of the usual gap between the two is unknown. The console figure is an estimate from the auction records, while the paid figure reflects invalid-traffic deductions, settlement timing, currency conversion, and any post-auction adjustment, and without a reconciliation a shortfall that signals a tracking loss or a billing error cannot be told apart from the normal settling of accounts. {{voice_recommend}} pulling the console revenue and fill alongside the payment statements and the ad-server fill for the same closed period, matching them by demand partner where the statements allow, and recording the gap and what explains it. After the reconciliation, track that gap each period so a change in it is caught early rather than noticed when a deposit looks low.

audit_checks.title: Report dimensions and timezone are not aligned across systems
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A figure from Amazon Publisher Services can only be compared against the ad server or a payment statement when both are counting the same thing over the same window. A timezone or a date-range definition that differs between systems shifts impressions and revenue into the wrong day, and a metric defined differently, such as a served impression against a measured one, counts a different event, so a comparison built on mismatched definitions produces a discrepancy that is an artifact of the setup rather than a real loss.

educate.base:
Two reports can describe the same inventory and still disagree, not because either is wrong but because they are not counting the same thing over the same window. Amazon Publisher Services (APS), the ad server, and a demand partner's statement each define their metrics and their reporting period in their own terms, and a comparison only holds when those definitions line up. When they do not, the difference between two reports is an artifact of the configuration rather than a real gap in revenue or fill.

The timezone is the first place definitions drift. APS reports against a configured timezone, and the ad server reports against its own. When the two differ, impressions and revenue near the edge of a day land on different calendar days in each system, so a single day's totals will not match even though the full-month totals do. Reconciling on a window of a few days, or aligning both systems to the same timezone before comparing, removes that mismatch.

The metric definition is the second place. A served impression is counted when the ad is delivered, while a measured or viewable impression is counted only when it meets a rendering or viewability threshold, and the Media Rating Council (MRC) defines those thresholds precisely. Comparing a served-impression count from one system against a measured-impression count from another compares two different events. Revenue has the same trap because one system can report gross revenue and another net of a fee, so a figure has to be matched against its like before a gap means anything.

The date-range basis is the third. A report run on the moment an impression was served can differ from one run on the moment revenue was booked because invalid-traffic filtering and late-arriving conversions adjust earlier days after the fact. A report pulled before those adjustments settle will not match the same report pulled a few days later, so reconciliation works best on a closed period that has had time to finalize.

A few signs point to dimensions and timezone that are not aligned:
  • The APS reporting timezone and the ad server timezone are set differently, and daily totals are compared without accounting for the offset
  • A served-impression count from one system is compared against a measured or viewable count from another as though they were the same metric
  • Gross revenue from one report is matched against net revenue from another without adjusting for the fee
  • Reports are pulled before invalid-traffic filtering and late adjustments have settled, so the same period reports different totals on different days
You may want to confirm that the report dimensions, the timezone, and the metric definitions match before any figure is compared across systems. One approach is to align both systems to the same timezone, confirm that each metric being compared is defined the same way, and reconcile on a closed period that has had time to finalize.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, traffic peaks at particular hours, so a timezone mismatch concentrates its effect at the edges of the day when those peaks straddle midnight in one system but not the other. You may want to align the APS timezone to the ad server's before comparing daily figures, and reconcile on a multi-day window so an hour-level offset does not read as a revenue gap.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, campaigns and promotions often run to a specific local calendar, so a report whose timezone does not match that calendar can split a promotion across two reporting days. You may want to confirm the reporting timezone matches the calendar the campaigns are planned against, so the figures line up with the periods the business actually measures.

learn_more.source_title: Optimizing Header Bidding Integrations on Transparent Ad Marketplace

educate.learn_more_note: Amazon Publisher Services's documentation on the report dimensions, metric definitions, and timezone settings available in its console is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the timezone, the metric definitions, and the date-range basis in each system before comparing figures, and confirm the reconciliation runs on a closed period.

aps_console.label: Amazon Publisher Services console
steps: Open the reporting settings and record the timezone the console reports against and the date-range basis it uses.
Note which impression metric each report column represents, such as a served impression or a measured one, and whether revenue is reported gross or net.
Pull a sample report for a closed period and record the dimensions used so they can be matched against the ad server.

ad_server_reporting.label: Ad server reporting
steps: Open the ad server reporting settings and record its timezone, its impression-metric definitions, and its revenue basis.
Compare those against the APS settings and note every place a definition or a timezone differs.
Run the matching report for the same closed period on aligned timezones and definitions, and record whether the figures reconcile once the definitions match or whether a real gap remains.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the timezone, the impression-metric definitions, the revenue basis, and the date-range basis for APS and for the ad server, and runs a matching report for a closed period on aligned settings. A setup where the timezones match or the offset is accounted for, where each compared metric is defined the same way, and where the figures reconcile once definitions are aligned passes. A setup where timezones or definitions differ and figures are compared without adjustment is a fail recorded with the dimensions where the definitions diverge.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the timezone, the metric definitions, and the date-range basis for each system above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} compares figures from Amazon Publisher Services (APS) against the ad server and payment statements without aligning the timezone, the metric definitions, and the date-range basis first. A timezone that differs between systems shifts impressions and revenue into the wrong day, and a metric defined differently, such as a served impression against a measured one, counts a different event, so a discrepancy built on mismatched definitions is an artifact of the setup rather than a real loss. {{voice_recommend}} aligning both systems to the same timezone, confirming that each metric being compared is defined the same way and that revenue is matched gross against gross or net against net, and reconciling on a closed period that has had time to finalize. After the alignment, recheck the figures so any gap that remains reflects a real difference rather than a definition that did not match.
